videos.title: TOP 10 Things to do in Skopje,  North Macedonia 2023!
videos.description:
TOP 10 Things to do in Skopje,  North Macedonia 2023!
 Skopje,  North Macedonia is an amazing place to visit in North Macedonia . If you want to know top 10 what to do in Skopje or you need a travel guide, please keep watching.

#skopje #northmacedonia 
 
 
5. Visit the Museum of the Macedonian Struggle
Visiting the Museum of the Macedonian Struggle in Skopje is a great way to learn about the history of Macedonia's struggle for independence and the key figures who played a role in this important chapter in the country's history. The museum features a collection of artifacts, photographs, and documents related to the Macedonian struggle, including weapons, uniforms, and personal belongings of the fighters. Visitors can also learn about the cultural, political, and social context of the period through interactive exhibits and multimedia presentations. The museum provides a comprehensive and informative overview of a critical period in Macedonian history and highlights the bravery and determination of the people who fought for their country's independence. Whether you are a history buff or simply interested in learning more about this important chapter in the region's history, a visit to the Museum of the Macedonian Struggle is definitely worth your time.
 
 
 
 
4. Explore the Kale Fortress
Exploring the Kale Fortress in Skopje is an essential activity for anyone interested in history and architecture. The fortress dates back to the 6th century and has been reconstructed and expanded several times throughout its history. It sits atop a hill overlooking the Vardar River and offers breathtaking panoramic views of the city. The fortress is home to the Museum of the City of Skopje, which features a collection of archaeological artifacts, weapons, and traditional costumes from the region's history. Visitors can also explore the various towers and walls of the fortress and imagine what life must have been like for the soldiers who once defended the city from invaders. A visit to the Kale Fortress is an unforgettable experience that offers a unique glimpse into the rich and diverse history of Skopje and the surrounding region.
 
 
3. Go to the Skopje Aqueduct
Going to the Skopje Aqueduct is a unique and exciting activity that allows visitors to experience an impressive engineering feat from the Ottoman era. The aqueduct is located just outside the city and was built in the 16th century to supply water to the city. It spans over three kilometers and is made up of 55 arches, many of which still stand today. Walking along the aqueduct provides a stunning view of the surrounding landscape and a chance to marvel at the engineering skills of the people who built it. The aqueduct is also a popular spot for hiking and picnicking, offering a peaceful and scenic escape from the city. For those interested in history, architecture, or outdoor activities, a visit to the Skopje Aqueduct is a must-do experience that offers something for everyone.
 
 
 
 
2. Visit the Church of St. Clement of Ohrid
Visiting the Church of St. Clement of Ohrid in Skopje is a fascinating experience that allows visitors to explore one of the city's most important religious and cultural landmarks. The church dates back to the 13th century and is dedicated to St. Clement, the patron saint of Macedonia. The church features beautiful frescoes and intricate wood carvings that depict scenes from the Bible and the lives of the saints. Visitors can also see the tomb of St. Clement, which is believed to contain his remains. The church is a beautiful example of medieval Byzantine architecture and is considered one of the most important cultural treasures of Macedonia. For those interested in history, art, and religion, a visit to the Church of St. Clement of Ohrid is a must-do experience that offers a unique insight into the rich cultural heritage of Skopje and the surrounding region.
 
 
 
 
 
 
1.   Take a day trip to Matka Canyon
 Taking a day trip to Matka Canyon in Skopje is an unforgettable experience that offers breathtaking natural beauty and a chance to explore some of the region's most stunning landscapes. The canyon is located just outside the city and features a beautiful blue-green lake surrounded by towering cliffs and lush forests. Visitors can take a boat ride along the lake, hike along the many trails, or explore the various caves and grottos in the area. The canyon is also home to several monasteries and churches, including the famous St. Andrew's Monastery, which dates back to the 14th century. Whether you are looking for adventure, relaxation, or simply a chance to connect with nature, a day trip to Matka Canyon is a must-do activity that offers something for everyone.

In this Skopje Travel Guide, I explore the real experience of visiting Skopje North Macedonia — from the famous Old Bazaar and Stone Bridge to local food, hidden gems, and travel tips. After visiting more than 58 countries, Skopje was one of the most surprising destinations I’ve seen. This is the real Skopje travel experience.

This Skopje travel guide covers top attractions like Old Bazaar, Stone Bridge, Macedonia Square, local food, budget tips, and hidden gems. If you're planning a Balkan trip, this Skopje North Macedonia vlog will help you decide where to go and what to expect.

videos.transcript: Welcome to our Skopje Travel Guide—everything you need to know before visiting North Macedonia’s quirky and captivating capital. From cultural landmarks and local food to day trips and budget tips, this video covers it all to help you plan the perfect trip. Don’t forget to like, comment, and share this video, and subscribe to my channel for more travel guides—if you have any questions, feel free to ask me in the comments below! Skopje, the capital of North Macedonia, is located in the heart of the Balkans and serves as a cultural, historical, and political center of the country. The city is known for its unique mix of Ottoman, Byzantine, and modern architecture, with grand statues and neoclassical buildings lining the Vardar River. One of the city's highlights is the Old Bazaar, one of the largest and most vibrant in the Balkans, offering a true glimpse into local culture. Skopje is also the birthplace of Mother Teresa and home to a wide variety of museums, historic mosques, and churches. For travelers, it offers an intriguing blend of East and West, old and new, and is a fascinating destination for history lovers and culture seekers. Skopje is easily accessible via Skopje International Airport, which is about a 25-minute drive from the city center. It is also conveniently located for travelers coming by road from neighboring countries like Serbia, Kosovo, Albania, Greece, and Bulgaria. On our trip, we picked up a rental car directly from the airport and used Skopje as the starting point of our 12-day Balkan road trip, which i mentioned company in description. Buses also operate from nearby capitals, making it a great hub for overland travel across the region. For those who prefer comfort, private transfers are available and can be a hassle-free option, especially if arriving late. Skopje’s city center is quite compact and easily walkable, especially around Macedonia Square, the Old Bazaar, and the Stone Bridge area. For getting around faster or to farther neighborhoods, local buses are affordable and widely used by locals. Taxis are inexpensive compared to other European cities, and we used them occasionally for convenience. While we mostly walked during our time in the city, electric scooters and bicycles are becoming more common and are fun alternatives. Renting a car is ideal if you plan to explore nearby places like Matka Canyon or venture beyond Skopje for day trips. The official currency is the Macedonian Denar (MKD), and while euros are sometimes accepted in touristy spots, it’s best to carry local cash. Macedonian is the official language, but many young people speak English, especially in the hospitality sector. North Macedonia is visa-free for many nationalities, including EU and US passport holders, but it’s good to check the latest entry rules before visiting. Skopje has a strong café culture, and hospitality is an important part of the local identity—expect warm welcomes and generous portions at restaurants. Also, dress modestly when visiting religious sites and always ask before photographing locals, especially in more traditional areas. Start your journey in Skopje Old Town, where cobbled streets and centuries of history create an atmospheric introduction to the city. Wander into the Old Bazaar, one of the largest and most vibrant in the Balkans, filled with traditional shops, tea houses, and local life. Enter through the Bit Pazar Gate, a symbolic entrance to the historic commercial district. Dive into the colorful chaos of the Bit Pazar Market, where you’ll find everything from fresh produce to secondhand treasures. Visit the peaceful Murat Pasha Mosque, one of the city's finest examples of Ottoman architecture. Step into Cifte Hammam, now a contemporary art gallery, where the mix of ancient hammam design and modern exhibitions creates a fascinating contrast. Take a break at Kapan Inn, a 15th-century caravanserai that now hosts restaurants and cafes with a historic ambiance. Admire the elegant Arasta Mosque, a quieter but beautiful corner of Skopje’s religious heritage. Walk through the Bezisten, a covered Ottoman-era market that retains its historic charm. Visit the grand Mustafa Pasha Mosque, offering one of the best panoramic views over the Old Bazaar. Suli Inn, built in the mid-15th century by Isa-Beg Isaković, is another notable caravanserai situated in Skopje's Old Bazaar. Kuršumli Inn is a 16th-century Ottoman caravanserai located in Skopje's Old Bazaar, originally serving as a resting place for traders and their animals. Discover the Church of the Ascension of Jesus, where the carved wooden iconostasis is a masterpiece of Balkan religious art. Explore Skanderbeg Square, honoring the Albanian national hero with a bold statue and modern surroundings. Climb up to Skopje Fortress, where ancient walls and sweeping views of the city await. See the National Gallery of Macedonia (Daut Pasha Hammam), housed in a former Turkish bath with a remarkable blend of history and fine art. Visit the Orthodox Church St. Demetrius, known for its peaceful interior and colorful frescoes. Stroll past the Fountain of Philip II of Macedon, which lights up beautifully at night. Pause by the Fountain Olympia - Mother of Alexander III Of Macedonia, another tribute in the city’s celebration of historical legacy. If you're a music lover, catch a performance at the Philharmonic of the Republic of Macedonia, home to world-class concerts. Right nearby, the State Archive of the Republic of Macedonia stands as a quiet but important institution preserving national memory. For a deeper understanding of tragic history, visit the powerful Holocaust Memorial Center for the Jews of Macedonia. Continue your journey to the Museum of the Macedonian Struggle for Independence, where immersive exhibitions tell the story of the nation's turbulent past. Spot the decorative Horses Fountain, often overlooked but photogenic and lively. Walk across the iconic Stone Bridge, connecting the Ottoman old town with modern Skopje and serving as the city's symbolic heart. Take in the sights along the Vardar River, where cafes, bikers, and river views come together. Find yourself at Macedonia Square, the city's main hub, surrounded by towering monuments and grand architecture. Gaze up at the enormous Warrior on a Horse Monument, one of the tallest equestrian statues in Europe. Admire the Ristic Palace, a graceful building that reflects Skopje’s early 20th-century charm. Walk through Gate Macedonia, the city’s triumphal arch inspired by classical European forms. Pay homage to a global icon at the Mother Theresa Memorial House, built on the site where she was born. Visit the serene Church Temple "Holy Equal-to-the-Apostles Emperor Constantine & Empress Elena", located in a peaceful residential area. Step inside the Museum of the City of Skopje, housed in the former railway station that was partly destroyed in the 1963 earthquake. Visit the Archbishop's Cathedral St. Clement of Ohrid, the largest Orthodox cathedral in Skopje with impressive murals and domes. Look up at the Telecommunication Center Building, a massive brutalist structure that defines Skopje's skyline. Finally, end your walk at the Government of the Republic of North Macedonia, an impressive neoclassical building that symbolizes the modern face of the city. When visiting Skopje, take time to wander through its historic cobblestone streets where every corner holds a piece of the past. Stop by traditional craft and souvenir shops to support local artisans and bring home something unique. Find a shaded courtyard café and sip on a strong Turkish coffee while soaking in the city’s ambiance. Don’t miss trying local street food and freshly baked Balkan pastries that fill the air with tempting aromas. Step inside old Ottoman-era buildings that now host modern art exhibitions—it’s a beautiful contrast of heritage and creativity. If you’re up for something unique, experience a traditional Macedonian hammam and let yourself relax in an ancient wellness ritual. In the evening, enjoy a lively Macedonian dinner night with authentic food, wine, and traditional music performances. Join a guided walking tour to uncover the rich and complex history layered throughout the city. Make time for a nature escape with a day trip to the dramatic gorges and caves of Matka Canyon. And if you're curious about cross-border culture, take a short day trip to Pristina for a glimpse into the capital of Kosovo. Skopje offers a fascinating blend of cultures shaped by its Ottoman, Byzantine, and Yugoslav past, and you’ll notice this in everything from its architecture to its daily lifestyle. The city is home to both Christian Orthodox and Muslim communities, and you’ll often hear the call to prayer alongside church bells. Locals are generally warm and hospitable, but it’s polite to greet people with a smile and avoid loud or confrontational behavior in public spaces. You should try a traditional Macedonian meal with rakija, experience a folk music night, and if possible, attend a local celebration or religious festivity. It’s respectful to dress modestly when visiting religious sites and to remove your shoes when entering someone’s home. Skopje can be visited year-round, but each season offers something different. Spring (April–June) is ideal with pleasant temperatures and blooming nature, while summer (July–August) can be hot but vibrant with open-air events. Autumn (September–October) is a favorite for many travelers due to its mild weather and colorful scenery. Winter (November–March) brings a quiet atmosphere and occasional snow, making the city cozy and peaceful, especially around the holidays. If you want to combine sightseeing with day trips to Matka Canyon or mountain villages, late spring and early autumn are the most comfortable. Compared to major European capitals, Skopje is very affordable and even cheaper than many other Balkan cities like Belgrade or Dubrovnik. A decent meal in a local restaurant usually costs around €6–€8, and a cappuccino is around €1.50. Mid-range hotels cost about €30–€50 per night, while luxury stays rarely go over €100 per night. Attractions and museum entries are budget-friendly, often costing between €2 and €5. The only thing that might feel a bit pricey for some is organized tours, but even those are generally under €30 per person. Yes, Skopje is an excellent destination for budget-conscious travelers. You can find hostel beds starting from €10 per night or private rooms for around €20. Many of the best experiences, like exploring the Old Bazaar, walking the riverside, or visiting local markets, are completely free. Traditional bakeries sell delicious burek or pastries for as little as €1–€2, and city buses are affordable, with a single ticket costing under €1. Even cultural attractions like art galleries and museums often offer free entrance days or discounts for students and seniors. In general, Skopje has a relaxed and modern dress code, but it’s good to dress modestly when visiting religious sites such as mosques and churches—covering shoulders and knees is recommended. During summer, light and breathable clothing is ideal as temperatures can get very hot, while winters are cold enough to require coats, boots, and layers. Spring and autumn are mild, so a mix of light jackets and comfortable layers work well. It’s also practical to wear comfortable shoes, especially for walking through the cobbled streets of the Old Bazaar and exploring the fortress. There are no strict clothing rules in daily life, but dressing respectfully will always be appreciated. The main area of Skopje is centered around Macedonia Square, which is surrounded by iconic monuments, fountains, and neoclassical buildings. The riverside promenade and Stone Bridge connect the city center with the Old Bazaar, making this whole zone the heart of tourism and culture. Most of the must-see attractions like museums, galleries, and statues are within walking distance here. It’s also where you’ll find bustling cafes, shops, and great photo spots both day and night. For first-time visitors, staying or spending most of your time in this area makes exploring Skopje both convenient and enjoyable. Skopje is a great place to enjoy traditional Balkan and Macedonian cuisine with rich flavors and hearty portions. Be sure to try grilled lamb, cevapi(grilled minced meat), beef stew, sautéed mushrooms, and fresh salads topped with cottage cheese. Burek, a flaky pastry filled with cheese or meat, is a local favorite for breakfast or a quick snack. For a vibrant dining scene, head to Kazandžiska Street in old town, filled with cozy restaurants, and don’t miss dining at Chardak Restaurant at the other part of the city for an authentic experience. While meat dishes dominate, you’ll also find vegan-friendly restaurants in the city, and for drinks, try local rakija or ouzo. Skopje is a great place to shop for handmade souvenirs, especially in the Old Bazaar where artisans sell copperware, filigree jewelry, and traditional embroidered fabrics. Local food products like honey, ajvar(pepper spread), and Macedonian wines make for perfect gifts to take home. Ceramics, wooden crafts, and traditional musical instruments are also popular choices for something unique. Bargaining is not very common in formal shops, but at open markets or small stalls, it’s acceptable to ask politely for a small discount. These local items are not just souvenirs—they reflect the culture and craftsmanship of Macedonia. Skopje offers a range of accommodation options from luxury hotels near Macedonia Square to boutique stays and budget-friendly Airbnbs around the Old Bazaar. The city center is the best area to stay for easy access to major sights, restaurants, and nightlife. We stayed at Villa Nina Hotel, which was great value for money at €55 per night for three people, with clean rooms and a warm local feel. There are also charming boutique hotels that blend modern comfort with traditional design, perfect for those who want a more intimate experience. If you prefer self-catering, plenty of stylish and affordable apartments are available for rent across the city. Skopje has a surprisingly diverse nightlife scene with something for everyone—from cozy wine bars to lively clubs. For a laid-back evening, you can relax in bohemian-style bars or enjoy live acoustic music in atmospheric venues. If you're into dancing, head to one of the clubs around the city center, where DJs play Balkan and international hits. There are also more authentic spots where locals gather to drink rakija and listen to traditional music. Whether you're looking to party or just unwind with a glass of wine, Skopje offers a warm and fun evening vibe. Skopje is perfect for both short and long stays depending on your travel style. If you're on a quick trip, one full day is enough to see the main highlights like the Old Bazaar, Stone Bridge, and Macedonia Square. For a deeper experience and to enjoy museums, riverside walks, and nearby day trips like Matka Canyon, I recommend staying 2 to 3 days. The city’s walkability and compact size make it easy to explore at a relaxed pace. Skopje also works well as a base for a wider Balkan road trip, like we did during our 12-day itinerary. Skopje is generally very safe for travelers, including solo visitors and women. People are friendly, helpful, and used to tourists, and we felt comfortable walking around both day and night. Like in any city, it's wise to watch out for minor scams such as overpriced taxis—use apps or confirm prices in advance. Petty theft is rare, but keeping your belongings secure in crowded areas is always a good habit. In case of emergency, you can dial 112, which connects to all essential services including ambulance and police. Absolutely Skopje is a unique destination that blends rich history, diverse culture, and quirky architecture. It offers an affordable and authentic travel experience with fewer crowds than other European cities. The mix of Ottoman heritage, socialist-era monuments, and bold new structures gives the city a character you won’t find elsewhere. Delicious local food, welcoming people, and easy access to nature make it even more appealing. If you're looking for a Balkan destination that’s different, budget-friendly, and full of stories, Skopje is definitely worth a visit. That wraps up our complete Skopje Travel Guide—packed with tips, must-sees, and local experiences to make the most of your visit. If you found this video helpful, don’t forget to like, comment, and share it, and make sure to subscribe to my channel for more adventures across the Balkans and beyond. And if you still have questions or need more recommendations, feel free to drop them in the comments—I’ll be happy to help!

Petty theft is rare, but keeping your belongings secure in crowded areas is always a good habit

videos.title: Skopje, North Macedonia 🇲🇰 Exploring a Hidden Balkan Gem (Travel Vlog)
videos.description:
Carrying on from Lake Ohrid, Kim and I took a 3 hour bus ride up to Skopje. 

If you want to travel around the Balkans by bus, you can use the following website: https://travel.gjirafa.com/en

Skopje was a really unique city, in a good way. Being the unofficial statue capital of the world, it definitely felt like a city like none other. If I had to compare it, it had a little mix of Bucharest and Budapest. (I love both of those cities!)

Skopje is extremely affordable, and I felt safe the entire time. Although there are no Uber's or Bolt's, there are taxi's everywhere around the city, so you'll be able to get around without any problems.

Visiting Matka Canyon is a must when traveling here. Designate at least 2-3 hours here, just to enjoy the nature side of Skopje. 

Between Lake Ohrid and Skopje, North Macedonia exceeded my expectations. I just loved it! I would definitely come back and recommend you to come as well 🇲🇰

#macedonia #northmacedonia #skopje #travel #travelvlog #vacation #wanderlust #fyp #europe #vlog #explore #adventure 

Instagram 📸 https://www.instagram.com/223.ransom/

videos.transcript: With only two nights in Scopier, we stayed at the Alexander Square Boutique Hotel for a total of $80. I think somebody owned this unit and just rented it out cuz besides the bathroom, it looked like it was never renovated. Despite looking outdated, I booked this place cuz it was right outside the main square. Neither Uber or Bolt are available in Scopier, so you'll have to rely on taking a taxi or bus if you decide not to walk a lot. That 80ft statue is the main reason why I wanted to come here. Scapia is unofficially known as the statue capital of the world, but this statue is controversial and iconic as it's none other than Alexander the Great. Alexander the Great was born in Pella, which is in the northern region of Greece [music] called Macedonia. There's some Macedonians who say he's Macedonian, and there's some Greeks who claim he's Greek. North Macedonia gained its independence in 1991. Previously, it was a part of a larger country called Yugoslavia. Yugoslavia was a country from 1918 to 1991 and consisted of the following current countries: Slovenia, Croatia, Bosnia, Serbia, Montenegro, Kosovo, and North Macedonia. When North Macedonia first gained its independence, it was called Macedonia. Greece had a huge problem with that as the northern part of Greece is already called Macedonia. Greece felt threatened that eventually they'd want to claim that land as well as steal a part of their history. Greece blocked North Macedonia from joining NATO until they officially changed their name in 2019 from Macedonia to North Macedonia. This is very controversial and I personally don't know anything about this matter, so don't kill me in the comments. Just educate me. For lunch, me and Kim stopped at this nice restaurant called Pelister. I got a steak and she got a pizza. We also got dessert. Everything cost $35. I felt very safe walking around Scopier and everybody we encountered spoke English, so communication was never a problem. But like any other city, there were a decent amount of people asking for money on the street. They weren't aggressive, and we never got harassed or anything like that. This is the Archaeological Museum of North Macedonia. It costs about $4 to enter. After watching other travel vlogs in Scopier, a lot of people called this city weird or quirky. I actually like the city a lot cuz it was different in a good way. For one, I love how this statue's everywhere. It's definitely a unique city. It kind of has a mix of Bucharest, Romania, Budapest, Hungary, and a little Istanbul, Turkey. And most importantly, it's super affordable. This boat right here is actually a hotel. It's called Hotel Sagalia, and it's really beautiful inside. It costs around $100 a night to stay here. Sorry. >> Yeah. Yeah. >> Standing at almost 100 ft, this statue portrays Philip of Macedon. Not only was he once the king of Macedonia, he was also the father of Alexander the Great. First, >> when we first walked into the bazaar, I didn't realize how big it really was. It's like a giant maze. When you think it's about to end, you start seeing more stores and restaurants. I mean, there were a ton of restaurants to grab food at, and it all smelled so good. The prices were really affordable, as it ranged anywhere from $3 to $10 a person. You can get ice cream for under a dollar. I also happened to buy a can of beer at a small convenience store for 50ents. I mean, honestly, how can you beat it? [music] >> She look like she could be a fan. >> No, I'm not. No, that's Macedonia Square is by far my favorite square in Europe. At night, me and Kim sat here for hours eating ice cream and listening to live music. It was packed with people and was just a good vibe overall. to I never knew about this place. This was definitely our spot for these two days. We probably got like six gelades each day. And these pastries were so good, I felt like a fatty eating them. This is the Mother Teresa Memorial House. Mother Teresa was born in Scapier and is known for her lifelong work with the sick and poor. She won the Nobel Peace Prize in 1979. >> [music] >> Down. Falling. [music] The next day, we woke up and got ready to go to Matka Canyon. It was about 30 minutes away, so we hopped in a taxi. I think I paid around 20 to $30 round trip. The taxi only took us so far, so we had to walk the rest of the way. Matka Canyon is free to enter. There's hiking trails as well as restaurants on the lake, so you can easily spend a couple hours here. We ended up doing a 1-hour boat cruise, and it cost $7 a person. You can also rent a kayak for $5 an hour. >> [music] [music] [music] [music] [music] [music] [music] >> After getting dropped off in the main square, we made our way to the Scottbier Fortress. It was a short walk, but I think Kim would have preferred to have been dropped off at the entrance. The current structure of the fortress was built in the sixth century. In 1963, an earthquake caused significant damage. In 2010, the southwestern wall was fully restored. It's definitely worth checking out, especially since it's free to enter. Down in the bazaar, we had walked by this restaurant the day before. It looked mad good, so we had to check it out. Donuts range from $3 to $6. It was a 10 out of 10. videos.title: 10 Things You MUST KNOW Before Visiting Skopje, North Macedonia
videos.description:
Traveling to Skopje? While it may not be well known by many travelers, it's a welcoming and unique capital city in the Balkans that is well worth a visit! This video is sharing the top 10 things you must know before going to Skopje in order to have the best trip possible. 
 
🇲🇰 My Skopje Travel Guide (currently free!): https://www.exploringwithcourtney.com/travel-guides/p/skopje-travel-guide-for-first-time-visitors

Thanks for watching :) Please like, comment, and subscribe!

videos.transcript: If you're visiting Scopia, North Macedonia, there are a few things that you really need to know before you go. From drinking tap water safely [music] to encountering the stray dogs literally everywhere, I'm breaking down everything so you don't show up unprepared. Scopia is a fairly small Balkan capital that's easy to overlook, but honestly, it became one of my favorite cities in the [music] region after my 2 months of backpacking through Eastern Europe. It's quirky with a lot to offer. And once [music] you know these 10 tips, I'm sure you'll love it just as much as I do. Today, we're covering safety, the language barrier, prices, getting around town, [music] things to do, and so much more. But first, if you're new to the channel, I'm Courtney, and I just quit my corporate job to travel the world solo for 1 year. So, if you want to join on the adventure and get my best travel tips, be sure to hit that subscribe button. All right, let's start off with topics that are top of mind for most travelers. Number one, do people speak English? The official language in Scopia is Macedonian, but yes, anyone dealing with tourists on a regular basis speaks [music] English very well. So, bus drivers, waiters, shop owners [music] all speak English. And actually, according to Education First's English proficiency index, Scopia ranks very [music] highly in English proficiency, coming in ahead of more popular European cities like Brussels, Paris, and [music] Prague. So, in short, I never had any communication issues, and you shouldn't either. Number two, is Scopia safe? Yes, generally speaking, Scopia is considered [music] to be a safe city. Even as a solo female traveler, I felt completely safe throughout my entire stay. Of course, follow common sense safety measures like hanging out in popular areas if you're going to go out at night, making sure your phone is charged and always has a good data connection since you shouldn't rely on public Wi-Fi. Personally, I used an eim from Olafly, [music] and the data connection was always reliable, so I highly recommend them. If you want to use them, be sure to use this code at checkout or follow the link in the description box for a discount. [music] Number three, what is the currency and what is the exchange rate? The currency is the Macedonian dinar. Please [music] excuse my pronunciation, but 50 dinar is equal to about 1 USD. [music] And yes, you will need cash on hand because many places require it. The good news is that there are ATMs all over the place. And these pink ones were always my go-to after a local told me that they had the lowest ATM fees. And more good news is that the cost of things in Scopia is pretty low overall. A [music] typical restaurant meal might cost you between $5 and $10, and a good quality hotel right in the center of town is typically between about $50 and $100 per night. Number four, can you drink the tap water? Something [music] I always appreciate while traveling is when the tap water is safe to drink. Fortunately, that is the case in Scopia. And like in most other Balkan countries that I visited, there are public drinking fountains all over the city. I found these so charming. [music] They're safe to drink from and they're really going to help you beat the heat in the hotter months. So when you see one of these, don't be afraid to take a sip. By the way, if you're enjoying this video or if you've discovered something new, please give it a big thumbs up to help other viewers find it more easily. All right, now that we got the quick ones out of the way, let's move on to the layout of the city and how to get around so that you have your bearings as soon as you arrive. Scopia is comprised of two separate districts, the old town and the new town, and they're divided by the Vardar River. The old town [music] dates all the way back to the 12th century and quickly became the city's commerce [music] hub. As you walk around it today, you'll see many historic mosques, winding cobblestone narrow alleys, and several jewelry shops and [music] restaurants, which is all pretty typical for the old town in a Balkan city. Scopia's old town is also home to the Scopia fortress, which you can enter for free and walk along the walls to get a nice overlook of the [music] city. The new town on the other side of the river is, of course, more modern with big roads and a real mix of architectural styles among the buildings. It was rebuilt in 1963 [music] after a destructive earthquake and underwent a major and controversial makeover in 2014 after the government decided to refresh building facades and erect those tens [music] and tens, maybe even hundreds of giant statues that you will notice all around the city. Many people [music] now find the appearance goddy and overdone, but it undeniably makes Scopia very unique. [music] I definitely recommend starting off your trip with a free walking tour so you can be led through both parts of the city by a knowledgeable guide. [music] The tour I went on was with Guru Walk and I've added a link below in the description box. [music] Number six, in terms of getting around the city, there is no need to rent a car because Scopia's public transit system is very easy and affordable. One bus ticket is 50 Macedonian DNA, which again is about 1 USD. [music] You can just buy it from the driver as you board the bus. And did you think that London was the only city with iconic red double-decker public buses? [music] Think again because Scopia's got them, too. So, if you've always wanted to ride in one, this is your chance to do it for cheap. Now, let's talk about those stray dogs that I mentioned at the [music] beginning of the video. As soon as you start walking around Scopia, you will notice all the stray dogs roaming the streets. Even as a dog lover, this really caught me off guard and immediately gave me flashbacks to when I was in Bali being chased down the street by packs of stray dogs barking menacingly. But thankfully, the dogs in Scopia are the exact opposite. They are the sweetest, most chill dogs ever. You'll notice the tag in their ear, which lets you know that even though they are strays, they've been vaccinated and neutered. Of course, still take precautions to not make the dogs feel threatened and do not feed them or honestly give them like too much attention unless you want them to be following you around for the rest [music] of the day. I made this mistake when I got a little too close to one with my camera and it started following me all the way back to my hostel and even waited outside of the grocery store for me when I stopped to get some food. Despite this, they were still super sweet and never made me feel uncomfortable. And let's wrap up with some must-do activities while you're in Scopia. My top recommendation has got to be spending at least a half day at Matka Canyon. This is a canyon just an hour outside of Scopia that makes for a really great nature getaway from the bustle of the city. You can easily get there by catching the public bus number 60. Matka Canyon features walking trails and a [music] lake where you can kayak or take a boat ride. I rented a kayak for just $6 an hour and I took it all the way out to the cave in the back of the canyon. This was a [music] workout and I was out there for like two hours. It was really fun and definitely a [music] highlight of my trip. The canyon is also lined with restaurants, so it makes for a really great spot for some Instagram pics [music] if that's something that you're interested in. Another activity that you should check out is shopping at a local market. You might be tempted to do all your shopping in the old town, but that's pretty touristy. So, for a more authentic experience, I definitely recommend checking out the green market in the new town. This is an outdoor marketplace where locals shop and it's open every single day. Let me tell you, the vendors were so excited to see me as an American coming through the marketplace [music] and everyone wanted to talk to me. It was a really fun experience. And lastly, Scopia is the birthplace of Mother Teresa. So, naturally, you have to check out her museum when you're in town. It's a small museum, so it won't take you long to go through and entry is free. Thanks so much for watching. If you enjoyed this video, give it a big thumbs up and drop a comment letting me know if you have any other questions about Scopia. I've got more Balkans travel videos coming soon, so be sure to subscribe to my channel so you don't miss those. And I'll see you [music] in my next video. Bye.
